old_title: Ukraine war live: Chinese nationals among 10 injured in drone attack on Moscow
new_title: Ukraine war live: Zelensky sacks armed forces chief in biggest military shakeup
old_text:
At least three Chinese nationals have been wounded in a major Ukrainian drone attack on Moscow, Chinese state media announced this morning.
The attacks were carried out over Sunday night and Monday morning and included 400 drones, targeting the Russian capital and its surrounding region.
Separately, India also confirmed four casualties to its nationals in a Russian attack on a ship leaving the southern port city of Odesa in Ukraine.
Ukraine said Russia hit the Golden Leo - a Guinea-Bissau-flagged ship manned by a crew from India and Syria - with three cruise missiles on Sunday.
This comes as Volodymyr Zelensky said he spoke with the new British prime minister Andy Burnham, shortly after he took office yesterday in London.
Burnham has promised to Zelensky that Britain's support for Ukraine would remain unchanged under his leadership, just hours after he replaced Sir Keir Starmer in No 10.
Burnham speaks to Zelensky and Trump in first calls after taking office
Andy Burnham has spoken with Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelensky and US president Donald Trump as part of his first set of phone calls with world leaders as prime minister.
Burnham promised that Britain's support for Ukraine would remain unchanged under his leadership, just hours after he replaced Sir Keir Starmer in No 10.
Zelensky confirmed the call on his Telegram channel and said the two sides reaffirmed their unwavering support for Ukraine and agreed to speak on bilateral cooperation in the near future, including within the Anti-Ballistic Coalition.
"I was happy to speak with Andy Burnham and congratulate him on taking office as prime minister of the United Kingdom. I truly appreciate that the support for Ukraine and our people will remain steadfast," he said.
Zelensky thanked Burnham for "warm words of respect for the Ukrainian people and for your condolences to the families and loved ones of those killed in Russia's recent attacks".
Trump said on Truth Social that he had spoken to Burnham in one of his first phone calls as PM. "The call was interesting, and went very well," Trump wrote.
Burnham, who has little previous foreign policy experience, has said that Britain will remain a firm United States ally.
IMF approves $690m for Ukraine after review
The International Monetary Fund's board completed a review of Ukraine's financing arrangement, clearing the way for immediate access to about $690m, the Fund said on Monday.
IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva said on Monday that maintaining macroeconomic stability remained Ukraine's immediate priority and called for prudent fiscal and monetary policies as well as measures to safeguard financial-sector resilience.
The war-torn nation is operating under an $8.1bn IMF loan programme.
The latest funding brings total disbursements under the programme to about $2.2bn, the IMF said in a statement.
The review is a key test for Ukraine as it struggles to sustain economic stability and advance reforms while battling intensified Russian attacks on its infrastructure.
The IMF said Ukraine had maintained macroeconomic and financial stability despite Russia's war.
However, it said the economic outlook had weakened largely due to intensified attacks on critical infrastructure and spillover effects from the war in the Middle East.
To keep the programme on track, IMF staff and Ukrainian authorities agreed in June to a revised timeline for implementing reforms, corrective actions to address slippages and additional policy commitments, the IMF said in June.
Why young Ukrainians in the streets want wartime military general gone
Thousands of angry protesters in Ukraine have taken to the streets triggering the largest protests the war-hit nation has seen since Russia's invasion in 2022.
For several days in a row now, these protesters have defied martial law to protest against last week's removal of young reformer Mykhailo Fedorov as defence minister and to demand the dismissal of Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi.
The protests have exposed deep divisions in the defence leadership on how to stop Russia's battlefield advances, as the war's overall momentum has swung in Kyiv's favour.
A poll from Gradus research company showed that 61 per cent of Ukrainians were against the dismissal of Fedorov.
Zelensky said he spoke with Fedorov, 35, "at length" over the weekend as the tussle between the two sides intensified, with the ousted young defence minister refusing to return to the government.
A source close to Fedorov told Reuters that he had discussed other roles with Zelensky, but that he was only ready to return as defence minister.
Ukrainian media reported that Fedorov had been offered roles overseeing military technology and innovation.
Dmytro Koziatynskyi, a war veteran and one of the organisers of the protests, called on supporters to launch a "general, indefinite protest" unless Syrskyi is removed and Fedorov reappointed as defence minister by 24 July.
Syrskyi, 60, faces criticism from civil society, soldiers and lawmakers for his Soviet-style command culture and high casualty tactics.
Zelensky meets military commanders amid street protests against army chief
President Volodymyr Zelensky met some of Ukraine's top military commanders on Monday to try to ease growing discontent with the defence leadership that has triggered the largest street protests since Russia's invasion in 2022.
Ukraine's military commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, 60, is facing mounting criticism from civil society, soldiers and lawmakers for his Soviet-style command culture and high casualty tactics.
Zelensky met five generals and one colonel, viewed by political analysts as potential replacements for Syrskyi, one after another to discuss the battlefield situation as fighting rages along more than 1,200km (746 miles) of the frontline.
The six most well-known and respected military commanders, including Commander of the Joint Forces Mykhailo Drapatyi and Deputy Chief of the General Staff Volodymyr Horbatyuk, represent a younger generation of Ukrainian officers whose careers were shaped in combat against Russian troops.
Oleh Apostol, commander of the air assault forces, and commanders of the three army corps - Andriy Biletsky, Ihor Obolenskyi, and Denys Prokopenko also met Zelensky, who posted pictures from his study following each meeting.
Zelensky's chief of staff, Kyrylo Budanov, called for unity and restraint in the wake of the protests, promising a constructive response.
"The public's views have been heard," he said. "Work is underway. Results will follow."
North Korean and Russian foreign ministers hold strategic talks in Moscow
North Korea's foreign minister Choe Son Hui and Russian foreign minister Sergei Lavrov held their third strategic dialogue in Moscow on Monday, North Korean state media KCNA said this morning.
The two sides "reached a consensus on all the issues brought up for discussion", the report said, with talks focusing on implementing their comprehensive strategic partnership treaty signed in June 2024.
Russia expressed full support for North Korean measures to counter what KCNA described as hostile acts threatening its position and right to development, as well as its sovereign rights and security interests.
North Korea expressed invariable support for Russian policies aimed at eliminating what it called the "root cause" of the conflict in Ukraine and defending Russia's sovereignty and territorial integrity.
Analysts have been watching for signs that Choe's visit could pave the way for another trip to Russia by the North Korean leader Kim Jong Un, who last travelled there for a summit with Putin in 2023.
Putin invited Kim to visit again during their June 2024 summit in Pyongyang.
North Korea has also been shoring up ties with China, with successive high-level exchanges this month marking the 65th anniversary of the countries' friendship and mutual defence treaty.
Chinese nationals injured in drone attack on Moscow
At least three Chinese nationals have been reported injured during a Ukraine drone attack on Moscow, Chinese state media announced this morning.
The Chinese embassy in Russia said two people were receiving treatment at a local hospital and were in stable condition without life-threatening injuries, while another had asked to be discharged for treatment back in China.
The embassy said it immediately activated its emergency response mechanism and asked Russian authorities to take all necessary measures to treat the injured and protect the safety and property of Chinese citizens.
The three Chinese nationals were among 10 people injured in an attack carried out over Sunday night and Monday morning.
Four Indian seafarers killed in 'Russian strike' on ship leaving Ukraine
Four Indian nationals were among 10 people killed in an alleged Russian cruise missile strike on a cargo ship leaving Ukraine's southern port of Odesa over the weekend.
India's foreign ministry said on Monday that the vessel, MV Golden Leo, was attacked on Sunday evening just as it was departing Odesa with 17 crew members, including five Indians.
The surviving Indian crew member was critically injured, the foreign ministry added.
The Ukrainian navy alleged that the vessel, flying the Guinea-Bissau flag and owned by a Turkish company, was targeted by Russian forces with three cruise missiles.
Ten people were killed, including a Ukrainian pilot, Ukraine's Sea Ports Authority said after launching a search and rescue operation.
Russia expels two Italian diplomats, foreign ministry says
Russia said it had declared two Italian diplomats persona non grata, calling it a reciprocal measure in response to the expulsion of two Russian embassy staff in Italy.
Italian foreign minister Antonio Tajani said on 9 July that Rome had expelled two military attaches at the Russian embassy in Italy who were allegedly involved in espionage activities.
The Russian foreign ministry said in a statement that Vittorio Parrella, the assistant defence attache at the Italian embassy, and Davide D'Aprile, an attache, were persona non grata and must leave Russia with their families within three days.
The ministry also said it had summoned Italian interim Charge d'Affaires Giovanni Scopa in connection with the expulsion of Russian embassy staff.
"The Russian Federation has expelled, without reason, the Italian military attache in Moscow along with one of his collaborators. This is an act of blatant retaliation for the expulsion from Italy of two Russian military attaches, who were indeed caught red-handed while carrying out espionage activities to the detriment of our national security," Tajani wrote on X on Monday.
Andy Burnham to pledge he will 'be there' for Zelensky on first call as PM
Andy Burnham has pledged he will "be there for" Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelensky and the UK's support would remain "resolute" under his premiership.
The new Prime Minister indicated that the President of Ukraine would be one of the first foreign leaders he would speak to after replacing Sir Keir Starmer in No 10.
He also indicated plans for a call with US President Donald Trump.
Mr Burnham has little foreign policy experience from his time in government, but he said he had worked with Ukrainian mayors while he was running Greater Manchester.
He said he would support Mr Zelensky personally, as well as offer the UK's backing to Ukraine.
Mr Burnham told broadcasters: "Those will be my first calls, and I'll be making very clear later today to President Zelensky that there is no change.
Burnham speaks to Zelensky and Trump in first calls after taking office
Andy Burnham has spoken with Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelensky and US president Donald Trump as part of his first set of phone calls with world leaders as prime minister.
Burnham promised that Britain's support for Ukraine would remain unchanged under his leadership, just hours after he replaced Sir Keir Starmer in No 10.
Zelensky confirmed the call on his Telegram channel and said the two sides reaffirmed their unwavering support for Ukraine and agreed to speak on bilateral cooperation in the near future, including within the Anti-Ballistic Coalition.
"I was happy to speak with Andy Burnham and congratulate him on taking office as prime minister of the United Kingdom. I truly appreciate that the support for Ukraine and our people will remain steadfast," he said.
Zelensky thanked Burnham for "warm words of respect for the Ukrainian people and for your condolences to the families and loved ones of those killed in Russia's recent attacks".
Trump said on Truth Social that he had spoken to Burnham in one of his first phone calls as PM. "The call was interesting, and went very well," Trump wrote.
Burnham, who has little previous foreign policy experience, has said that Britain will remain a firm United States ally.
new_text:
Ukraine's president Volodymyr Zelensky has sacked the head of the armed forces and named his successor, after a week of high pressure from his citizens including mass protests nationwide.
Zelensky bowed to the pressure and fired Gen Oleksandr Syrskyi after mass demonstrations across Ukraine demanded his removal and the reinstatement of former defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov, who was fired last week.
Protesters insisted that Syrskyi embodied the army's old, Soviet-style command culture, while Federov was seen as a driver of military modernisation.
The Ukrainian leader has since vowed that "deep strikes" on Russia will continue under his replacement, Mykhailo Drapatyi.
Fedorov has subsequently been offered a "respected position" in government to unify the country's technnology sector, Zelensky said. It was not clear whether the former defence minister accepted, or if this would bring the unrest to an end.
Earlier, Moscow launched an attack on a Ukraine-bound gas tanker near Romania's coast, as the Kremlin vowed to step up its attacks on ships supplying Kyiv's military.
Meanwhile, Romanian president Nicusor Dan said the Liberian-flagged vessel sailing from Egypt to Ukraine was hit in an attack likely linked to Russia's war on Ukraine.
Who is Mykhailo Drapatyi, Ukraine's new army chief?
Major General Mykhailo Drapatyi was named the new commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian armed forces late on Tuesday as President Volodymyr Zelensky sought to reset his military strategy in the war with Russia.
Drapatyi, the commander of the joint forces responsible for the Ukrainian defences in the northeastern direction, is described by his colleagues as a general of a new generation whose career was shaped in combat against Russian troops.
During his 26 years in the army, he rose through the ranks from a tank lieutenant to one of Ukraine's most effective combat commanders. Last week, in a rare public post, he said the Ukrainian army needed changes and new rules.
Drapatyi has fought Russian troops in Ukraine for more than a decade. He became famous in 2014 during fighting in the Donbas when he shouted "Go!" at the driver of an armoured fighting vehicle in Mariupol and tore through a Russian barricade on a mission to rescue besieged police officers. That same year, he led an encircled force that fought its way through Russian territory to freedom.
After Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022, Drapatyi became the go-to commander for areas of the front where the situation was most dire and in need of a swift turnaround. In summer 2024, he stopped a Russian incursion near the city of Kharkiv, stabilised the front line, and soon after became the youngest-ever commander of the Ukrainian land forces, one of the most senior posts in the Ukrainian army.
Ukraine agrees to export drones to the US
Ukraine has agreed to export drones to the United States to participate in a drone dominance program, a source tells Reuters.
The agency is reporting that six Ukrainian firms have been given permission to export to the US.
Ukraine widens strikes on 'logistics hubs' in push to disrupt Russia's war effort
Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelensky said on Wednesday that Kyiv has struck what he described as logistics hubs in Russia's Krasnodar and Stavropol regions involved in supplying Russian military with drone components and other equipment.
An oil depot, a tanker and four cargo ships from Russia's "shadow fleet" were also hit in Black Sea and the Sea of Azov, Zelensky added.
Separately, the head of Russia's largest online retailer, Wildberries, said another attack on its warehouses had injured serveal people.
Tatyana Kim said the warehouses in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk had been attacked. Social media footage showed a huge plume of smoke rising into the air in Krasnodar.
Krasnodar regional governor Veniamin Kondratiev also said that 10 people were wounded in an attack on a storage complex, while drone debris has damaged two high-rise apartments in Krasnodar, where no one was injured.
The attacks on a company so central to the consumer economy appear to mark a widening of Kyiv's strategy of using long-range drones to disrupt Russia's war effort and pressure the Kremlin to make peace.
Ukraine hit 13 Russian vessels in past 48 hours, drone force commander says
Ukrainian drones struck 13 Russian vessels in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov in the past 48 hours, the commander of Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces Robert Brovdi said on Wednesday.
Catch up: Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires military chief after protests and names a replacement
President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Tuesday fired Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces, following days of mass protests in Kyiv and other cities nationwide demanding his removal.
Zelenskyy appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new army chief, according to a statement on social media.
"Our shared wish is one - victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace," Zelenskyy said. He said "a significant path has been traveled, the defense of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment."
Read the full story:
Italy aims to reach 5% Nato target in the next decade
Italy's foreign minister Antonio Tajani has set out a timeline for Italy to reach its Nato target of spending 5 per cent of GDP on defence.
He says the country wants to reach the target within the next decade.
How Ukraine's new commander-in-chief forged his reputation fighting Russia in 2014
The new commander-in-chief of the Ukrainian military forged his reputation as a daring and determined fighter in the early days of the country's battle against Russia, then spent a decade in combat assignments while steadily rising through the ranks.
His reputation was forged in 2014, early in Russia's war in eastern Ukraine.
That May, as a major commanding a battalion of the 72nd Brigade, he led an armored column into central Mariupol to rescue police officers and territorial defence officials besieged by pro-Russian militants who had threatened to burn them alive.
When the column's path was blocked by a barricade more than a metre high, Drapatyi, riding in the lead vehicle, ordered the driver to accelerate, and the vehicle bounced over the barrier as if off a ramp.
The moment, which was captured on video that circulated widely, is considered one of the most remarkable episodes in the early resistance.
It was a bold move for the Ukrainian army at the time, when decisive and independent moves by field officers were rare. The army was still poorly equipped, largely untested and wary of any action that might escalate tensions with Russia.
Military reshuffle in Ukraine celebrated in Russia
Russian war bloggers responded to Ukrainian defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov's dismissal with glee, noting his role in masterminding long-range drone strikes on oil facilities that have caused a fuel crisis and midrange attacks that disrupted military logistics.
President Volodymyr Zelensky dismissed the popular young upstart minister Fedorov last week, sparking protests across the country.
Bowing to the public pressure, Zelensky yesterday fired Ukraine's commander-in-chief General Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered Fedorov a position in government that he said would unify the country's technology sector.
It wasn't clear if the offer was accepted and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters.
Syrskyi, some noted with grudging respect, has built a string of defences that have stalled Russian advances.
Many Moscow commentators voiced hope that the controversy would fuel political infighting in Ukraine and weaken its military.
Zelensky names new military commander: Everything we know so far
Volodymyr Zelensky has named Major General Mykhailo Drapatyi to lead Ukraine's war efforts as commander-in-chief and fired General Oleksandr Syrskyi following days of protests in Kyiv and other cities demanding his removal.
Drapatyi forged his reputation as a daring and determined fighter in the early days of the country's battle against Russia, then spent a decade in combat assignments while steadily rising through the ranks.
Aged 43, he represents a new generation of Ukrainian generals forged in the crucible of the country's fight against Russia.
He has spent nearly his entire career in combat postings, including leading Ukraine's Joint Forces, which direct day-to-day combat operations across branches of the army.
He is known as intensely disciplined and highly effective in combat, and has previously been sent in to stabilise the most critical sectors of the front.
He has rarely appeared in public but has nonetheless gained respect and popularity within the military and beyond for his past victories.

Mr Burnham has little foreign policy experience from his time in government, but he said he had worked with Ukrainian mayors while he was running Greater Manchester. He said he would support Mr Zelensky personally, as well as offer the UK's backing to Ukraine. Mr Burnham told broadcasters: "Those will be my first calls, and I'll be making very clear later today to President Zelensky that there is no change. Burnham speaks to Zelensky and Trump in first calls after taking office Andy Burnham has spoken with Ukraine's Volodymyr Zelensky and US president Donald Trump as part of his first set of phone calls with world leaders as prime minister. Burnham promised that Britain's support for Ukraine would remain unchanged under his leadership, just hours after he replaced Sir Keir Starmer in No 10.
